23.1.label: Question 23.1
23.1.scenario:
A long‐term insurance company is about to review the premiums for its without‐profits term

assurance product that pays a fixed benefit only on death within a certain term. At the same
time, it is going to add a critical illness benefit. It has not previously offered critical illness cover
on any of its contracts, although other insurance companies in the market do so. The full sum
insured will be payable on the first of death, or diagnosis of a specified critical illness, within a
term of ten years.
23.1.question:
Explain how policy and other data relating to the mortality and critical illness benefits may be a
source of risk to the insurer in these circumstances.

Solution: It is referring to model and parameter risks, ie the choice of model and the choice of parameters.
The risk is that the unit fund at retirement age, net of any charges, is less than the value of
premiums accumulated at 3% pa as the insurer would then have to make up any shortfall.
This effectively means that the unit fund must grow on average at a rate rather larger than 3% pa
in order to offset the charges on the funds and the insurer may not achieve this rate.
Even if the extent of the guarantee appears fairly low, there must be some risk that the insurer
will have to find some money to cover a shortfall in the value of the unit fund. The expected cost
Initially, the insurer should thoroughly research the possible candidates before entering into any
agreement to use their services.
Once the insurer has decided on a provider, it should make sure that the agreement itself is
watertight, in terms of who is allowed to do what under what conditions etc. This may include
fixing fees at a specified level, thereby helping to control the insurer’s expenditure.
Once the agreement is in place, the insurer should monitor the experience and investigate
anything unexpected early on. Regular spot checks and visits to the third party might help.
(i)        Anti‐selection on individual IP contracts
Individuals who know they are ill and likely to claim in the very near future may seek cover. There
may also be a tendency for those who are currently in very good health to think that they do not
need this type of insurance, but the impact of this will be less important.
(ii)       Anti‐selection on group IP contracts
Anti‐selection risk is generally reduced on group IP contracts, because the individuals involved
have less active choice about taking out the contract.
There is also, in the case of individual contracts, a sickness risk from selective lapses.
There is also a risk under IP contracts of moral hazard – where, as a result of having insurance, the
policyholder behaves in such a way as to increase the insurer’s claim costs (see Chapter 7).
(i)       Moral hazard on IP policies
IP policyholders who are receiving benefits have less of an incentive to return to work than others
in similar situations who are not covered by insurance. Therefore those with insurance might
tend to delay their return to work more than those who are not covered. In effect, the existence
of the insurance alters the behaviour of the individual, resulting in a financial cost to the insurer.
(ii)      Transition probabilities affected by moral hazard
The transition rate from sick to healthy (recovery) is reduced by the effect of moral hazard. It may
also affect claim inception rates, in that uninsured persons will be more likely to struggle on at
work, even while ill, than those who can legitimately claim benefits under their policies.
The design of the contract – Some contract designs could be made more capital efficient than
others and this could be quite significant. For example, contracts with reviewable premiums
would be more capital efficient than contracts with guaranteed premiums.
Premium frequency – Individual contracts are usually regular premium, but this would be a
significant factor if single premium contracts were available.
Initial expenses – These could be significant, because of heavy underwriting. The additional
complexity of the contracts may also increase other acquisition costs. For example, it may be
necessary to pay higher commission to intermediaries.
Solvency capital requirement – This could be very significant (particularly if the reserving basis
does not use prudent assumptions), because the greater unpredictability of sickness rates
compared with death rates increases the risk.
The main problem would be the unknown, and theoretically unlimited, claim amount. It would
encourage the policyholder to have the most luxurious and expensive care possible.
The capital requirements could be comparable to those for endowment assurance, or other
types of investment contracts, but will depend on the nature of the contract and in particular
any guarantees that are given.
An endowment assurance is a contract that pays a benefit on maturity or on earlier death.
The similarity to the endowment as regards capital requirements stems from the fact that a LTCI
contract has the same pattern of paying regular premiums in advance, for a benefit that (when
payable) will be paid from the point at which premiums stop.
It can perhaps be considered as even more similar to a whole life assurance (where the benefit is
paid on death whenever that might be), as there is no fixed latest time at which the claim has to
be paid. But unlike an endowment or a whole life assurance, the benefit under a LTCI policy is not
certainly paid, as policyholders can die without needing any long‐term care.
With guarantees come risks, and increased risk is likely to require increased prudence and hence
larger reserves (or larger solvency capital). Some supervisory authorities may specifically require
additional reserves / capital to be established to cover certain types of guarantee.
Policy data: The insurance company has no policy data for the critical illness benefit.                                      [½]
It may have reasonably good data for mortality, based on its own recent experience.                             [½]
However, using such data would pose risk:           the data are inaccurate or incomplete                                                                [½]
          the volume is insufficient for any calculated rates to be reliable                                   [½]
          any key features determining the rates on existing data have changed or are expected to
It is also possible that the addition of the critical illness benefit will make the product appeal to a
different market, thus changing the mortality experience.                                             [½]
In any case, mortality rates calculated from the policy data would have to be adjusted to allow for
the fact that on some deaths no payment will now be made (because the contract ended with
Other data: Therefore the insurance company may have to rely on other data to set its mortality assumptions.
It will certainly need to do so for the critical illness assumptions.                                           [½]
Possible sources would include reinsurers or industry data (if available).                                      [½]
Depending on the market, these data might be unreliable or incomplete, ...                                      [½]
... although there should be a reasonable volume in well‐established markets.                                   [½]
Data may be unrepresentative of the insu:           the possible variety in policy conditions for the critical illness benefit, ...                      [½]
... especially in the diseases covered and the definitions of illness                                [½]
          the different target markets reached by different insurance companies                                [½]
          the different levels of underwriting and claims management that may be employed.                     [½]
The insurance company can try to adjust the data to allow for its own circumstances, …                          [½]
… but there is still a major risk of getting this wrong …                                                       [½]
… especially for critical illness, where there is no own data at all for guidance.                              [½]
There is also a risk that any data used will not be appropriate for use in the future due to changes
... for example, the impact of screening programmes on critical illness claims.                  [½]
Syllabus objective: 3.1        Assess how the following can be a source of risk to a health and care insurance
0 Introduction: This chapter continues looking at the sources of risk to a health and care insurer.
1 Guarantees and options: In the case of both guarantees and options, the insurance company is offering terms in
advance of the happening of the event to which those terms relate. The actuary will need to
determine the cost to the company of providing these guarantees and options, using
parameters for the future experience in respect of the assumptions relevant to the particular
situation, combined with a model of how to calculate that cost. There will then be risks
Question: A health and care insurer offers a regular premium, unit‐linked pre‐funded long‐term care
insurance policy, with the long‐term care benefits being available from normal retirement age.
The insurer guarantees that the fund proceeds at retirement age from the contract will not be less
than premiums paid accumulated at 3% pa.
State precisely the additional risk that the insurer faces as a result of giving this guarantee.
A health insurer has decided to add a renewal option to its ten‐year without‐profits CI contracts.
It will charge for this option by increasing the premium for the original contract to cover the
expected cost of the option. Has the risk for the insurer increased by giving this option?
What could be done to mitigate the risk of using third parties for claim handling?
Describe a simple multiple‐state model that might be used for IP contracts.
(i)        Explain the nature of the anti‐selection risk on individual IP contracts.
(ii)       Explain why anti‐selection risk is generally lower under group contracts.
(i)        Explain how moral hazard is most likely to occur on a portfolio of IP policies.
(ii)       State which transition probabilities will be affected by the moral hazard and state how
they will alter.
In addition to the difference between the pricing and the reserving bases, what factors will affect
the capital requirements of individual IP business, and how significant would you expect each
factor to be?
Explain the effect that medical advances might be expected to have on future CI rates.
Describe a multiple‐state model that might be used for LTCI contracts.
What is the problem with using a benefit definition of the ‘cost of care needed’?
The insurer would use a model to determi: It might be possible to use a deterministic model, however, as a deterministic model is a
projection of one possible outcome, then (depending on the choice of parameters), the guarantee
may appear to have no cost.
It would be possible to use a range of deterministic scenarios, ranging from pessimistic to
optimistic, and to assign some probabilities to each outcome. The insurer could then take an
expectation based on this. This would be better, but would probably be highly subjective in terms
of the scenarios chosen and the probabilities assigned to them.
A better approach is likely to be a stochastic model. The insurer would have to choose a suitable
stochastic model along with suitable parameters.
The model would be run using simulations. This would produce several thousand possible
outcomes, and hence of the expected cost of the guarantee could be determined as an average of
all the simulated outcomes.
Even for a stochastic model, the results will only be valid if the model and the chosen parameters
are appropriate.
Health and care events are often less easy to predict than their life insurance counterparts
and greater care has to be taken in the wording of options and guarantees, the prices
charged for them and the reserves held to provide for their utilisation. The measurement of
the risk will vary directly with the relevance and volume of data used to assess its impact.
This extra uncertainty could be reflected in the models used. For example, extra prudence could
be built into the parameters used.
2 Competition: The need to compete, in a free market, may lead the management of an insurance company
to take decisions that increase its risk profile beyond that which can be supported by the
available resources. Some of the decisions that it might take are to:
          reduce premium rates or charges under new business contracts
          offer additional guarantees and options under new business contracts
          increase the coverage under existing contracts, eg further critical illnesses
          increase salaries or commissions in the respective distribution channels
          constrain the future growth of charges on existing business with reviewable
The impact of the above decisions can then be compounded if greater volumes of new
Equally, the actions of competitors can have adverse implications for new business
volumes and for the renewal of short-term health insurance policies. For example, cheaper
cover could be offered by competitors, which could result in policyholders selectively
Yes \u2013 there are several additional:           even though the insurer has charged for the expected cost of the option, the costing of
the option will involve a model and parameters that may turn out to be incorrect
          there may be unforeseen changes over the course of the ten years of the original contract
          there may have been weaknesses in the original underwriting that are compounded by
allowing customers to take out further contracts
          the increased premium might be viewed negatively in the market, leading to lower sales
It is of course not always the case that the example decisions given in the Core Reading above will
be inappropriate or will stretch an insurance company beyond its means. Where this is the case,
the actuary’s course of action will depend on local regulation and professional guidance, and on
3 Actions of management: An actuary of a health and care insurance company will make recommendations as to how it
should operate so that its risk profile stays within the resources available to it. However,
management may choose not to follow these recommendations:
\uf0b7 for competitive reasons (:          due to strategic company goals, such as maximising new business volumes or
amount of funds under management (including differentiating by market segment
\uf0b7 to maximise shareholder e: There is nothing in itself wrong with seeking to maximise shareholders’ returns. However, the
actuary may be in a particularly good position to judge the financial risks involved in a particular
The actuary may also have a regulatory or professional responsibility to ensure that inappropriate
risks are not taken, particularly where the security and fair treatment of policyholders are
There may in fact be a fundamental conflict between shareholders’ interests and those of
policyholders, because the two groups may have very different attitudes to the trade‐off between
expected profit and risk of insolvency. For example, a without‐profits policyholder has nothing to
gain from the insurance company pursuing high profits at high risk, but a great deal to lose if the
4 Counterparties: When an insurer enters into an agreement with another entity, it is relying on that entity to
meet its obligations under the agreement. There is a risk that the entity will not be able to
do this, and that it may either fully or partially default on its obligations or perform them to
an unacceptable standard; this is counterparty risk. The term ‘credit risk’ may also be used
The use of counterparties can also increase other types of risk due to the reduction in direct
4.1 Reinsurance arrangements: When an insurance company enters into a reinsurance agreement with either another
insurance company or a pure reinsurance company, there is a risk that the reinsuring
company will fail to meet its obligations, eg it may default on the payment of reinsurance
Reinsurance will be discussed more fully: Under reinsurance arrangements, it is usual that the full liability to the policyholder rests with the
insurance company that sold the business in the first place. The reinsurer’s liability is to the
insurance company, not to the policyholder. If the reinsurer is unable, perhaps because of
financial difficulties, to pay its share of a reinsured claim, then the insurance company faces a
The possibility of such a failure therefore represents a risk for the insurance company.
The risks here are that:           A distributor may be in a position, knowingly or otherwise, to commit the insurer to
conditions that were not the original purpose of the contract.
For example, a broker might (if allowed), in the course of selling a LTCI contract, manually
change the policy wording by making the ADLs more lenient, in order to satisfy a
demanding customer.
          A distributor, under circumstances where policyholder money transactions are
conveyed through the distributor, may not return premiums received at the
appropriate time, or may become bankrupt before these are passed over (ie credit
risk). A similar problem may arise with claims.
For example, the broker might hold on to all premiums paid to him/her and only pass
these on to the insurer at the end of every quarter. These represent very short‐term
assets to the insurer, but have the added complication of possible default.
At the other end of the spectrum, a valid claim payment may be made by the insurer to
the broker, to be paid in due course to the policyholder. It is possible that the broker
becomes bankrupt before the claim payment is finally made, in which case the insurer will
probably still be liable to pay the claim.
         A distributor may, in dealing with clients on behalf of the insurer, bring the insurer
into disrepute.
For example, the broker might dispute a claim directly with the policyholder, only for the
policyholder to find out later from the insurer, after complaining directly to it, that the
claim was perfectly valid.
The nature of the practical and legal relationship between the insurer and the distributor will
dictate the extent of these risks and the means to mitigate or resolve their impact.
For example, thorough training and established claims control procedures should mitigate the
example in the third risk above.
4.3 Provision of medical services: The area of health and care insurance is a complex one and often third parties are involved
to manage aspects of the client relationship and control claims cost. Examples of these are
underwriting agencies, third party claims assessors and disability counsellors. The extent
to which these bodies can commit expenditure on behalf of the insurer is a source of risk.
Under medical expenses covers and some long-term care insurances, the benefit itself is
provided by third parties on an indemnity basis. The risk of the ultimate claims cost then
lies, to at least some extent, in the hands of these third party providers. Insurers may have
some control or influence over these third parties (eg agreements with medical providers).
4.4 Investment: There is counterparty risk associated with some investments that the insurer may be
holding to back its business. This particularly relates to corporate bonds and deposits.
There is a risk that the issuer of the bond (or holder of the deposit) will default on its
obligations to pay coupons (or interest) and/or to repay the debt (or deposit).
5.1 Legal, regulatory and tax developm: Local law and supervisory requirements may often over-ride the explicit conditions
specified in the policy. New legislation and regulation may apply to policies already in
force, changing the nature of the contract between insurer and policyholder. For example,
certain exclusions may be deemed no longer acceptable or the payout for certain conditions
may be increased nationally by court precedent. Taxes may be introduced to benefits or
premiums that may change the profitability or coverage of existing contracts.
Another example of a legal restriction is the prohibition of certain factors to be taken into account
when pricing policies. For example, in some countries, local legislation prohibits the use of race as
a factor to be used to determine CI insurance premium rates.
LTCI is another type of health insurance that is likely to be the target for legal developments. It is
an unwritten rule (in most developed countries at least) that ‘society’ should take care of the sick
and elderly. So, often these contracts are subject to unexpected and sudden mandatory changes,
There’s not a lot that can be done about this. The best plan is to keep an ear to the ground to
listen out for any possible developments, and build a certain level of prudence into the
5.2 Reputation: Where there is a high degree of consumer market awareness or a culture of consumer
protection, the insurer runs the risk of losing both existing client base and potential new
business, as a result of obtaining a reputation for poor customer service. This may arise
formally and legally through the courts and regulators, but also less formally through press
comment, television exposure (consumer watchdog programmes), word of mouth and,
Again, the media generally like to concentrate on insurance ‘misdemeanours’, especially where
these occur against the sick or elderly. Therefore, health and care insurers often have a moral
obligation over and above their contractual policy obligations, sometimes arising quite
As we have already noted, there is also a reputational risk relating to counterparties used
5.3 Internal audit failures / fraud: Any business operation is at risk from internal misdemeanour or fraud, which is a type of
operational risk. Staff in positions of trust may abuse that privilege. This may take the form
of leaking of confidential information or the embezzlement of funds. Appropriate training,
governance and internal audit procedures are vital.
It is not uncommon to see press articles about possible fraudulent internal corporate activity, not
only within health and care insurers but in any corporation.
5.4 Physical risks: An insurer runs a number of risks to the physical conduct of its business, eg fire, flood, loss
of key staff. It is imperative to have business continuation procedures in hand to manage
the smooth flow of business in these circumstances, including systems back-ups and
alternative premises. Insurance cover will be needed for the event, including business
interruption cover, but the intervening damage makes proper processes and drills essential.
5.5 Aggregation and concentration of r: Part of an insurer’s assessment of portfolio risk will be the extent to which the insurer is
over-exposed to a particular risk or region as a result of specialisation of product or
concentration of distribution. Examples of this in health and care insurance might arise
where a small sickness insurer was at risk of an outbreak of local illness, or where an
insurer had concentrated on providing group income protection to an industry that was now
These risks are mitigated through more widespread marketing, through reinsurance and
Coinsurance with reciprocation is whereby risks are shared with another similar insurer (or
reinsurer), but one having risks of a complementary nature. For example, the ‘small sickness
insurer’ above, is at risk of an outbreak of local illness, could share its risks with another (perhaps
small) sickness insurer with a concentration of risks in another area of the country. By sharing
each other’s risks, diversification is achieved for both insurers.
5.6 Catastrophes: A health and care insurer is at risk from a catastrophe, ie an event that gives rise to the
introduction of widespread illness (eg epidemic, pandemic) or injury (eg war, terrorism). By
their very nature, these are difficult to predict. Their resolution lies mainly in reinsurance or
possibly in more global expansion to spread the risks.
A catastrophe in health and care insurance is normally an event that gives rise to many individual
claims, all stemming from the same cause (such as an epidemic or war). Insurers would not
normally get an individual immense loss. This fact will influence the type of reinsurance that an
Furthermore, policy terms and conditions may be written to specifically exclude claims
relating to risks caused by such events.
5.7 Non\u2010disclosure and anti\u2010: We introduced underwriting in Chapter 7. It is covered in detail in Chapter 27.
Non-disclosure, the decision made by the policyholder at the proposal stage not to share all
risk-related information with the insurer, is difficult to police and prove. It makes the
assessment of appropriate premiums more problematic.
The problem is possibly exacerbated if the insurer decides to accept all policyholders
initially on the basis of a declaration of health and then underwrite more formally at the
claim stage – the moratorium approach (see Chapter 27). Here the insurer will have greater
difficulty in proving knowledge at the time of policy application and it is often a cause of
media outcry if claims are refused at a time of medical need.
Despite this disadvantage of the moratorium approach, there are several advantages, in
          policyholders don’t like answering questions about their health, and will be more
attracted to insurance companies who do not ask them (at least at the proposal stage), so
          new business administration is simpler, and thus cheaper, for most policies. Therefore for
policies that never claim, this is a clear saving.
\uf0b7 clearly worded proposal:           more frequent use of doctors’ reports at new business stage
\uf0b7 thorough audits on sampl:           closer dialogue between underwriting, sales and claims management.
The topic of access to information from genetic tests is very relevant here and may be
subject to local legislation and practice. Note the use of genetic testing by health and care
insurers has been an area of considerable debate in many countries around the world. This
issue is covered in more detail in Subject SA1.
The use of genetic test results was intr: Anti-selection (and its management) has been discussed elsewhere in the course. The key
risk to the insurer is a greater degree of anti-selection than has been anticipated in the
premium rates, emphasising the important link between pricing and underwriting.
6 Product\u2010specific risks: The risks described below are the ones that are particularly important for each of the main types
of health and care insurance products.
6.1 Income protection insurance risks: The main risk relates to the sickness transition probabilities in the underlying multiple-state
model, ie the claim inception rates and the claim termination rates. This risk increases with
the relative leniency of the contract’s definition of what constitutes incapacity.
1. healthy (able to work): 2.        sick (unable to work according to the policy definition, and receiving benefit)
The possible transition probabilities ar: 1.         healthy to each of the ‘needing care’ states (known as claim inception rates)
2.         separate transitions from each ‘needing care’ state to progressively more dependent
states (ie State 2 to 3, State 2 to 4 and State 3 to 4)
3.         separate transitions from each ‘needing care’ state to dead (known as mortality from the
1. healthy to sick (known as clai: 2.        sick to healthy and sick to dead (known as claim recovery and mortality from the sick state
respectively, or collectively as claim termination rates)
3.        healthy to dead (known as mortality from the healthy state).
In order to include lapses in the model, an additional state of ‘lapsed’ would be needed, along
with an extra transition from healthy to lapsed. (There would be no transition from sick to lapsed,
as people who are sick would be receiving benefits and so would not benefit from lapsing.
Inception rates would have to be determined by age, and termination rates by age and duration
of claim. Further breakdowns would also be likely, such as by gender and smoking habit.
More complex models have been proposed,: the probabilities of transition between the states must be estimated in order to decide what
premium to charge, and the insurance company is at risk of getting these estimates wrong.
This problem is not helped by the fact that ‘unfit to work’ is a less well‐defined concept than
‘dead’. The less well defined the criteria are for making a claim, the more uncertain will be the
claims experience (and, therefore, the greater the risk).
If the sickness criteria in the policy conditions are more lenient, that is, where policyholders don’t
have to be quite so sick to claim under the contract, this will lead to a greater range of incapacity
This broader definition of incapacity wi: 1.         it will increase the expected cost of claims (because more people will qualify for benefit
2.         it will mean that the insurer will be able to estimate its expected sickness experience with
It is the second of these that increases the risk, as the increase in expected sickness rates can be
allowed for by increasing the premium rates charged.
Industry data is usually a fairly reliable source of data, however, because policy conditions vary so
much between different insurers, using the industry data from IP policies as a basis for obtaining
future sickness rate assumptions is a lot more difficult (and subject to much more uncertainty)
than the equivalent life insurance data for mortality.
Associated with this risk is a significant anti-selection risk in the case of individual
contracts and a minor risk in the case of group contracts.
There are lesser risks associated with m:          The main mortality risk is that claimants live longer than expected, ie claim termination
probabilities are lower than expected, for example, treatments for specific illnesses may
be successful in extending life rather than curing the illness. There is also a risk from early
deaths of healthy policyholders before the initial policy expenses have been recouped.
         The main expense risk is that expenses are higher than expected, leading to reduced
profits. Possible causes include higher than expected inflation and poor business
volumes, leading to high per‐policy expenses.
         The main investment risk is that investment returns on the assets backing the business
are lower than expected. (Note that reserves on IP insurance can be quite high, but are
not usually large enough for investment profits / losses to be significant compared with
Also lapses will be a financial risk at times when the asset share of the contract is negative.
This will generally happen early on in t: Capital requirements will depend on the relationship between the pricing and supervisory
reserving bases, in particular with regard to the sickness assumptions, but are likely to be
low relative to most other types of contract.
This will of course depend on the particular circumstances and the supervisory regulations. One
could argue that the need for prudent margins is greater for sickness contracts than for life
contracts, because of the greater difficulty in predicting sickness experience. Of course, this may
affect the pricing basis to some degree too.
6.2 Critical illness insurance risks: For both stand-alone and rider benefits, the main risk is in respect of the rates of diagnosis
of the critical illnesses specified in the contract.
A particular aspect of this is the relatively limited information available in most markets with
which to assess the likely rates of diagnosis. These contracts are fairly new to most markets and
the limited data mean that estimates of historical experience rates will be unreliable. Unreliable
estimates of historical CI rates will mean that estimates of future expected CI rates are equally
unreliable, leading to a high risk that actual CI experience is significantly worse than expected.
This parameter risk will increase the risk from these contracts considerably.
The problem is made worse if different insurers cover different illnesses under their CI contracts.
Any industry data could then be very heterogeneous, and so misleading as a basis for any
Unexpected changes in future rates of diagnosis also constitute a risk.
Medical advances might:           prolong life – for stand‐alone policies, this delays the time of death and so may lead to
more illnesses being diagnosed, causing more claims to be paid than before
          improve screening for diseases, such as for cancer – earlier identification of illness will
result in earlier claims being paid, increasing their cost to the insurer, and in some cases
making claims occur before the end of a policy term
         enable an individual’s susceptibility to certain illnesses (such as heart attack) to be
identified more readily, and the disease itself may be prevented (or its onset delayed),
thereby improving the claims experience.
Associated with this will be a significant anti-selection risk for individual contracts and a
much-reduced one for group contracts. In addition, for individual stand-alone contracts,
there will be a risk from selective lapses.
Even as a rider benefit, there will be some risk from selective lapses.
Insurance companies will of course seek to reduce the anti‐selection risk, for example by using
medical underwriting and/or a waiting period.
Stand-alone contracts will give rise to an expense risk and a usually minimal investment
risk:
         The main expense risks are similar to those for IP insurance.
         The investment risk would usually be small because, as would be expected from a
protection policy, the reserves would be relatively small.
A financial risk from lapses will also arise at times when the asset share is negative.
The capital requirements of a stand-alone contract or the marginal requirements of a rider
benefit will be of the same order as for an income protection contract.
This is likely to be the case because expenses and commission for the contracts will be of the
same order, and the supervisory requirements should be similar.
6.3 Long\u2010term care insurance risk: The main risk with such contracts relates to the transition probabilities in the underlying
multiple-state model. Of these the most important will be the claim inception probabilities
and any transition probabilities between claim states if there are more than one.
4. healthy to withdrawn: 5.         healthy to dead (known as mortality from the healthy state).
With LTCI contracts, it is generally safe to assume no ‘recovery’ transitions (as people normally
only deteriorate with age), although this cannot be ruled out completely. (Recovery transitions
could be included between the ‘needing care’ states if this were felt to be appropriate to the
individuals in question. Ignoring the reverse transitions is also the more prudent assumption.)
Another feature to note is that there should be the same number of ‘needing care’ states as there
In most markets there is currently a shortage of reliable data on which to base estimates of
transfer probabilities, because the contracts are relatively new. The contracts are relatively well
established in the United States, but US data may need significant adjustment before it could be
This is because experience would relate to entirely different populations and/or product designs.
\uf0b7 behaviour and environmen:           social attitudes to self‐help and insurance, eg in some cultures it may be the norm to
attempt to claim an insurance benefit at the first opportunity, whereas in others, there
may be an ethic of ‘keep going till you drop’
          policy wordings and coverage, which might reflect different levels of State provision.
Associated with these, there will be a significant anti-selection risk and a risk from selective
withdrawals.
Furthermore, there may be a problem of moral hazard, although this might be managed through
the use of ADLs.
There will be an investment risk as significant reserves may build up in advance of a claim
starting, and there will be an expense risk.
While LTCI is a protection policy and not every policy will result in a claim, a high proportion of
them will: many people need some form of long‐term care before they die. The high claim
inception rates will therefore lead to significant reserves, especially as the potential cost of the
benefits once claims have commenced can be very high.
At times when the asset share is negative, there is a financial risk from withdrawal. At other
times, whether there is such a risk depends on how any withdrawal benefit paid compares
with the asset share.
There are additional risks where the policy pays directly to the care provider and where the
policy indemnifies the cost of care.
If the policy pays directly to the care provider, then there is a risk that a payment is made upfront,
and then the care provider subsequently goes bankrupt. If the policy indemnifies the cost of care,
then there is a risk that care costs are higher than expected, perhaps due to higher than expected
care cost inflation.
There could be a significant marketing (and reputational) risk with these contracts, as the
policyholder may expect the benefits to be enough to cover the eventual costs of care.
With most benefit definitions, the insurer would not be guaranteeing to cover the total cost of
care, and so would have to be careful to make this clear to the policyholder. If the policyholder
was led to an inappropriate expectation that all care costs would be covered, then a failure to
meet the expectation could seriously harm the insurer’s public image, and would almost certainly
be bad for future sales of the contract.
6.4 Private medical insurance risks: The main risk to the insurer is that it may have limited control over the benefit payments.
An anaesthetist or consultant will make their own decisions regarding what they charge for
services rendered – although the insurer can impose some constraints through the use of
agreed fee schedules and hospital lists. The insurer may also have some ability to
influence the insured members to its preferred medical providers with whom it has
agreements.
Similarly, the original trigger for the claim may be the general practitioner referring the
patient for specialist advice, so claim frequency is very much bound up in this process of
referral – over which the insurer has no direct control.
The risk of anti-selection is an issue for individual business and small groups, with the
insurer typically mitigating this risk by performing medical underwriting at the policy
inception. However, this can still be a risk for health service requirements that can be
planned to some extent (eg pregnancy, dental and optical services).
In regions where the State offers an alternative (free) healthcare service, the insurer is
constantly under pressure to remind policyholders that the insurance package is preferable
to the ‘free alternative’. Failure to do this, especially in difficult economic times (and thus
when premiums become unaffordable for many), may result in the loss of considerable
business, with associated risks of inability to cover expenses and ultimate insolvency.
However, it is interesting to note that many PMI insurers still give cash benefits for using the
alternative free health and care service for some treatments, even though their insurance would
normally cover the cost of treatment. This keeps claims costs down, as the cash benefit will be
less than the cost of providing the treatment  and for many treatments the policyholder may not
care whether he or she received private or State treatment (eg for emergency, but minor
treatment, such as a minor fracture).
While most claims are modest in comparison with many commercial general insurance
risks, the private medical insurer is always at risk, where no policy limits apply, of a single
large claim (eg expensive treatment in the USA) or a single incident giving rise to an
accumulation of claims (eg accident within a single insured workforce).
However, most PMI policies do specify policy limits for most types of claim.
Capital strain under PMI is not usually as severe as under many of the long-term products.
This is because of its short‐term nature. Reserves need only be held in respect of a one‐year time
horizon, and so the possibility of something going horribly wrong is much reduced (although of
course the insurer would hope to keep the policy in force for much longer than one year).
Significant new business cost can, however, arise in scenarios where an insurer decides to
invest in the recruitment of an existing sales team and either a new starter ‘bonus’ is
payable or initial commission is promised that exceeds an annual premium.
This new business cost would arise whatever the contract type. Hopefully, the insurer will allow
for this extra cost in its business model, to ensure that overall, profits will ultimately meet
required targets. Clearly the idea of enhanced commission is to encourage sales and so it is
hoped that the profit on the greater volumes of business would pay (at least) for the extra
commission.
Guarantees and options: To calculate the cost of guarantees and options, an insurance company will use a model.
Competition: The need to compete may lead management to take unacceptable risks. This might involve
          reduce premium rates or charges under new business contracts
          offer additional guarantees and options under new business contracts
Actions of management: The company’s management may choose to ignore the actuary’s advice concerning what the
actuary views as unacceptable risk. Possible reasons for this are:
Counterparties under reinsurance arrange: If an insurer has reinsurance agreements with other insurers then it is at risk from the
possible failure of the reinsurers to cover their share of policy liabilities.
Regulatory and fiscal developments: Developments might relate to tax, policy conditions, exclusions and premium rating etc.
Reputational risks: Reputational risks might arise through media exposure, court cases and regulators.
Internal audit failures / fraud: Examples of internal audit failures / fraud are leaking of information and embezzlement of
Physical risks: Examples of physical risks are fire, flood, impact, loss of key staff.
Aggregation and concentration of risk: An example of aggregations / concentrations of risk is the outbreaks of local illnesses.
Non\u2010disclosure and anti\u2010select: Non‐disclosure makes premium rating more difficult. The extent of this risk depends in part
on whether a moratorium approach is used.
There is also a risk that anti‐selection is greater than anticipated in the pricing basis.
IP insurance:          claim inception and termination rates, including anti‐selection
Long\u2010term care insurance:           claim inception and transfer probabilities, including anti‐selection
\uf0b7 single large claims and:           capital strain is even lower than IP and CI, unless commission is high
